nav.policy_sub: How Conjur policy YAML is structured

hero.eyebrow: Palo Alto Networks | IDIRA · Secrets Manager · Kubernetes · ESO · DevSecOps
hero.title: Secrets Management
hero.subtitle: Four integration patterns. Zero hardcoded credentials. Spring Boot (direct JWT), .NET (Secrets Provider sidecar), GitHub Actions (OIDC), and a K8s-native ecommerce app using External Secrets Operator — all fetching credentials live from Palo Alto Networks | IDIRA Secrets Manager.

problem.title: Hardcoded credentials are a ticking time bomb

items.title: Credentials in source code
items.desc: Database passwords committed to git repositories leak in breach dumps, old forks, and CI logs.

items.title: No rotation
items.desc: Static credentials are never rotated. One leaked password = permanent access until manual intervention.

items.title: No audit trail
items.desc: Who accessed what secret, when? Without a secrets manager, you have no idea.

problem.solution_title: Dynamic secrets with Palo Alto Networks | IDIRA Secrets Manager
problem.solution_desc: Secrets Manager authenticates workloads using their Kubernetes identity (JWT) — not passwords. Secrets are fetched at runtime, cached briefly, and rotated automatically. No secret ever touches your code.

architecture.title: How Secrets Manager fits in Kubernetes
flow: Pod starts — Kubernetes projects a short-lived JWT token into the pod filesystem
App (or Secrets Provider) authenticates with Secrets Manager using the JWT
Secrets Manager validates identity against its policy and returns a short-lived API token
Secrets Vault surfaces the requested credentials to Secrets Manager, which enforces policy entitlements
App Container reads the secret values directly from Secrets Vault using the API token
App connects to MySQL using the retrieved credentials
Credentials are cached in memory — refreshed automatically before TTL expires
step_titles: K8s Projects the Service Account JWT

springboot.title: Spring Boot
springboot.label: authn-jwt / Java SDK
springboot.desc: The Spring Boot app integrates directly with Secrets Manager's REST API. It reads the Kubernetes JWT, authenticates with Secrets Manager, and fetches secrets on startup — with a 5-minute in-memory TTL cache.

steps.title: Authenticate with Secrets Manager
steps.code:
POST /api/authn-jwt/eks-latam/conjur/authenticate
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Accept-Encoding: base64
Body: jwt=<url-encoded-jwt>

steps.title: Fetch secrets
steps.code:
GET /api/secrets/conjur/variable/data/vault/.../username
GET /api/secrets/conjur/variable/data/vault/.../password
GET /api/secrets/conjur/variable/data/vault/.../address
Authorization: Token token="<api-token>"

steps.title: Cache & connect

springboot.live_note: Masked values — actual secrets never reach the UI

features.title: Direct API
features.desc: Calls Secrets Manager REST API directly from Java — no sidecar needed

features.title: TTL Cache
features.desc: SimpleTtlCache with double-checked locking — one Secrets Manager call per 5 minutes

features.title: JWT auto-renewal
features.desc: Kubernetes auto-rotates the projected JWT; Secrets Manager validates it on each auth

features.title: Typed errors
features.desc: JwtNotFoundException, AuthException, SecretFetchException with HTTP codes

dotnet.title: .NET
dotnet.label: K8s Sidecar / Zero-code
dotnet.desc: The .NET app has zero Secrets Manager code. A Palo Alto Networks | IDIRA Secrets Provider sidecar container handles authentication and injects credentials as Kubernetes Secrets — which the app reads as standard environment variables.

steps.title: Secrets Provider sidecar starts
steps.code:
# In K8s Deployment spec:
containers:
- name: panwidira-secrets-provider-for-k8s
  image: panwidira/secrets-provider-for-k8s:latest
  env:
  - name: CONJUR_AUTHN_LOGIN
    value: host/conjur/authn-k8s/...

steps.title: Provider authenticates with Secrets Manager
steps.code:
# Provider reads service account JWT automatically
# Authenticates via authn-k8s or authn-jwt
# Fetches: db_user, db_password, db_host

steps.title: Secrets written to K8s Secret
steps.code:
# Provider updates Kubernetes Secret 'db-dotnet'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-dotnet
data:
  username: <base64>
  password: <base64>
  address: <base64>

steps.title: .NET reads env vars — no Secrets Manager code

features.title: Zero app code
features.desc: The .NET app is completely unaware of Secrets Manager — reads standard env vars

features.title: Sidecar pattern
features.desc: Secrets Provider runs as a sidecar container, continuously syncing secrets

features.title: K8s Secrets mode
features.desc: Provider writes to a K8s Secret; app gets values via secretKeyRef in deployment

features.title: Any language
features.desc: Works for any app — Java, Go, Python, Ruby — without SDK changes

gha.title: GitHub Actions
gha.label: OIDC / Zero stored credentials
gha.desc: GitHub Actions pipelines authenticate with Palo Alto Networks | IDIRA Secrets Manager using GitHub's built-in OIDC JWT — no API keys, no stored passwords. The workflow's identity (repository + workflow name) is the credential.

steps.title: Enable OIDC token generation
steps.code:
permissions:
  id-token: write   # allow GitHub to issue OIDC JWT
  contents: read

jobs:
  demo:
    runs-on: self-hosted

steps.title: Call the Secrets Manager action
Secrets Manager policy enforces identity
steps.code:
# Conjur host must have matching annotations:
annotations:
  authn-jwt/github/repository: aslancarlos/workshop-action
  authn-jwt/github/workflow:   workshop-action
# Any other repo or workflow = access denied

steps.title: Secrets injected as masked env vars
steps.code:
- name: Connect to database
  run: |
    mysql -h "$DB_HOST" \
          -u "$DB_USER" \
          -p"$DB_PASS" \
          userdb -e "SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 5;"
# DB_USER / DB_PASS are auto-masked in all logs

stages.title: JWT Auth & Secret Retrieval — GitHub's OIDC JWT authenticates directly with Secrets Manager, no API keys
Multiple Secrets & Masking — fetch several secrets at once; values are auto-masked in logs
Database Connection — live MySQL connection using Secrets Manager-fetched credentials
Hardcoded vs Secrets Manager — hardcoded password fails, Secrets Manager credential succeeds
Least Privilege — authorized path succeeds, unauthorized path is denied by policy
Real Query & Data — end-to-end: Secrets Manager delivers creds → query runs → data returned
Credential Rotation — rotate in Privilege Cloud, re-run pipeline unchanged, new password works
Environment Promotion — dev → staging → prod with human approval gate on production
SSH Deploy with Key from Secrets Manager — key retrieved, used, deleted; never stored on runner
Docker Registry Login — registry credentials from Secrets Manager, docker login with --password-stdin
Audit Trail — query Secrets Manager audit API and display last 20 secret fetch events

features.title: Zero stored secrets
features.desc: No credentials in GitHub Secrets, runner env, or repository files

features.title: OIDC identity
features.desc: GitHub's JWT binds to repo + workflow — any other caller is denied

features.title: Least privilege
features.desc: Secrets Manager policy controls exactly which secrets each workflow can access

features.title: Full audit trail
features.desc: Every secret fetch logged in Secrets Manager — who, what, when, from which workflow

runner.title: Live workflow run
runner.pending: Workflow dispatched — GitHub hasn't reported the new run yet. It will appear here in a few seconds; press Run again if it doesn't.
runner.hint: Click Run to dispatch the workshop on GitHub. Each row updates every 3 seconds while the run is in flight. Backed by aslancarlos/workshop-action — rate-limited to 1 run per IP every 10 minutes.
runner.err_rate_limited: You triggered a run recently — please wait ~{{wait}} before triggering another.
runner.err_global_cap: Daily run cap reached for this demo. Try again tomorrow or open the repo on GitHub.

esoshop.title: ESO Shop
esoshop.label: ESO / K8s-native / Zero app code
esoshop.desc: A live Node.js ecommerce app that uses the External Secrets Operator to sync credentials from Palo Alto Networks | IDIRA Secrets Manager into a Kubernetes Secret — with a Python operator watching for secret changes and triggering zero-downtime rolling restarts automatically.

steps.title: ExternalSecret declares what to sync
steps.code:
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: eso-shop-db-creds
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1m
  secretStoreRef:
    name: conjur-store
    kind: ClusterSecretStore
  data:
    - secretKey: DB_USER
      remoteRef:
        key: data/vault/dev-demo-aslan/dbuser_dual/username
    - secretKey: DB_PASS
      remoteRef:
        key: data/vault/dev-demo-aslan/dbuser_dual/password
    - secretKey: DB_HOST
      remoteRef:
        key: data/vault/dev-demo-aslan/dbuser_dual/address

steps.title: ClusterSecretStore authenticates with Secrets Manager
steps.code:
# ESO uses springboot-app ServiceAccount JWT
# to authenticate via Conjur authn-jwt/eks-latam
provider:
  conjur:
    url: https://latamlab.secretsmgr.cyberark.cloud
    auth:
      jwt:
        account: conjur
        serviceID: eks-latam
        serviceAccountRef:
          name: springboot-app
          namespace: conjur
          audiences: [conjur]

steps.title: K8s Secret created — app reads env vars
steps.code:
# ESO creates and keeps the Secret in sync
# App deployment references it:
envFrom:
  - secretRef:
      name: eso-shop-db-creds
# App reads DB_USER, DB_PASS, DB_HOST
# as standard environment variables — zero Conjur code

steps.title: Python operator watches and restarts
steps.code:
# Operator streams secret watch events
for event in w.stream(v1.list_namespaced_secret, ...):
    rv = event['object'].metadata.resource_version
    if rv != last_rv:   # secret was updated by ESO
        trigger_restart()  # patch deployment annotation
# Rolling restart: maxUnavailable=0, minReadySeconds=15
# PodDisruptionBudget: minAvailable=2 of 3 replicas

esoshop.live_note: DB user shown as-is — no plaintext passwords exposed

features.title: ESO sync
features.desc: External Secrets Operator syncs Secrets Manager → K8s Secret every 60 seconds

features.title: Zero app code
features.desc: Node.js app reads standard env vars — no Secrets Manager SDK or sidecar

features.title: Auto-restart
features.desc: Python operator detects secret updates via Kubernetes Watch API and triggers rolling restarts

features.title: Zero downtime
features.desc: maxUnavailable: 0 + PodDisruptionBudget ensures at least 2 of 3 pods serve traffic at all times

esoshop.operator_title: Python operator: watch secret → rolling restart
esoshop.operator_desc: A custom Python controller running in-cluster watches the eso-shop-db-creds secret via the Kubernetes Watch API. When ESO updates the secret (after syncing a rotated credential from Secrets Manager), the operator detects the resourceVersion change and patches the deployment with a new annotation — triggering a rolling restart with zero downtime.

operator_steps.title: Watch Stream
operator_steps.desc: Streams list_namespaced_secret with field_selector on the target secret name — no polling.

operator_steps.title: Detect Change
operator_steps.desc: On MODIFIED event, compares resourceVersion. Only acts when it actually changed — ignores spurious events.

operator_steps.title: Rolling Restart
operator_steps.desc: Patches deployment annotation eso-operator/restartedAt. Kubernetes rolls pods one by one with maxUnavailable: 0.

flow.title: How Secrets Manager authenticates workloads
flow.subtitle: A step-by-step look at the JWT authentication and secret retrieval flow shared by all four integration patterns.
flow.s1_desc: Four clients with distinct Kubernetes identities: Spring Boot, .NET 8, GitHub Actions, and ESO Shop. Each authenticates independently using its own ServiceAccount JWT (or OIDC token for GitHub Actions).
flow.s2_desc: Palo Alto Networks | IDIRA Secrets Manager is the central identity broker and secrets vault, running at latamlab.secretsmgr.cyberark.cloud. All four patterns authenticate to this single control plane.
flow.s3_desc: Secrets Manager does not trust JWTs blindly — it forwards each token to the Kubernetes API Server for validation. The API Server confirms the token is genuine and bound to a real ServiceAccount.
flow.s4_desc: Spring Boot sends its Kubernetes ServiceAccount JWT to Secrets Manager's authn-jwt/eks-latam endpoint. Secrets Manager validates the identity against its policy and returns a short-lived API token.
flow.s5_desc: The Palo Alto Networks | IDIRA Secrets Provider sidecar — not the .NET app itself — authenticates with Secrets Manager using JWT. The application never handles any credential exchange directly.
flow.s6_desc: GitHub Actions uses its native OIDC token instead of a Kubernetes JWT. Secrets Manager validates the workflow identity: repository name and workflow name must match the policy exactly.
flow.s7_desc: The External Secrets Operator authenticates to Secrets Manager using the eso-shop-sa ServiceAccount JWT. It syncs credentials into a Kubernetes Secret every 60 seconds — the app reads only env vars.
flow.s8_desc: Once identity is confirmed, Secrets Manager retrieves the requested secrets from its internal vault: DB_USER, DB_PASS, and DB_HOST from the data/vault/dev-demo-aslan/dbuser_dual path.
flow.s9_desc: Credentials reach the application as environment variables. The app connects to MySQL using dynamic, Secrets Manager-managed credentials. No secret ever touches source code or a container image.

compare.title: Four patterns — which fits your use case?
compare.subtitle: All four use Kubernetes or OIDC identity — no static credentials, no hardcoded passwords.

best_for.title: Spring Boot — Best for
best_for.desc: Java services needing fine-grained control, dynamic refresh, and per-secret audit visibility directly in app code.

best_for.title: .NET — Best for
best_for.desc: Lift-and-shift migrations and polyglot services where zero SDK changes are required — any language reads env vars.

best_for.title: GitHub Actions — Best for
best_for.desc: CI/CD pipelines and deploy workflows outside Kubernetes — no cluster needed, identity comes from GitHub OIDC.

best_for.title: ESO Shop — Best for
best_for.desc: Cloud-native apps on Kubernetes wanting secret rotation with automatic rolling restarts and zero application code changes.

livetools.subtitle: All dashboards and apps live at demo.minha.cloud — accessible now, no setup required.
livetools.k8s_desc: Full cluster visibility — pods, deployments, services, secrets, CPU/memory graphs — in read-only mode. No credentials needed: click Skip to explore. Admin token available for write access.

livetools.dotnet_desc: Live .NET ecommerce app reading DB credentials from a K8s Secret injected by the Secrets Provider sidecar.
livetools.esoshop_desc: Node.js ecommerce app using External Secrets Operator to sync Secrets Manager credentials into Kubernetes Secrets.
livetools.controller_desc: Start and stop Kubernetes deployments across namespaces to reduce cluster costs when apps are not in use.

dual.title: Zero-Downtime Credential Rotation
dual.subtitle: Two accounts, one always active. A step-by-step animation showing how CyberArk Dual Accounts guarantee the Credential Provider always serves a valid credential — even during password rotation.
dual.s1_title: The Setup — Two Accounts, One Active
dual.s1_desc: In the Vault, Account A is tagged ACTIVE and Account B is tagged INACTIVE using the DualAccountStatus property. On the target system (DB/server), both accounts exist with identical permissions and are both enabled — the Vault status is the only switch.
dual.s2_title: Normal Operation — CP Serves Account A
dual.s2_desc: The application requests credentials from the Credential Provider. The CP checks its in-memory cache and returns Account A's credentials. No Vault call on every request. Account B sits idle, ready to take over.
dual.s3_desc: The Central Policy Manager detects that the rotational group is due for a password change based on platform settings. It prepares to flip the DualAccountStatus of both accounts. No passwords have been touched yet.
dual.s4_desc: CPM simultaneously updates DualAccountStatus: Account A becomes INACTIVE, Account B becomes ACTIVE. On the target system both accounts are still valid and enabled — no lockout, no error. The grace period begins now.
dual.s5_title: Grace Period — CP Refreshes Its Cache
dual.s5_desc: The Credential Provider detects the status change in the Vault and refreshes its cache. It now serves Account B. The grace period exists precisely to give every CP in the environment time to switch before any password is touched.
dual.s6_desc: The application continues requesting credentials. The CP now returns Account B's credentials seamlessly. Zero restarts, zero errors, zero downtime. The active account's password is never changed while it is active.
dual.s7_title: Grace Period Ends — CPM Resets Account A
dual.s7_desc: After the grace period, CPM rotates the password of Account A (now inactive) on the target system and updates the Vault. Account A gets a fresh, secure password. Account B continues serving all traffic undisturbed.
dual.s8_title: Cycle Complete — Ready for Next Rotation
dual.s8_desc: Account A has a rotated, secured password. Account B is active and serving all traffic. At the next scheduled rotation the process repeats in reverse: B→Inactive, A→Active, then B gets rotated. Always two accounts, always one active.
dual.key1_desc: The active account's password is never changed while it is active. Only the inactive account is rotated — and only after the grace period ensures every CP has already switched to the new active account.
dual.key2_desc: A configurable delay between the status flip and the password reset. Gives every Credential Provider in the environment time to refresh its cache and start serving the newly active account before any credential becomes invalid.
dual.key3_desc: Both accounts exist and are valid on the target system at all times. The Vault's DualAccountStatus is the only control — and it flips atomically with no window of unavailability.

jwt.title: How JWT Authentication Works
jwt.subtitle: From pod startup to secret retrieval — a step-by-step animation of how workloads authenticate to Secrets Manager using Kubernetes ServiceAccount JWTs without any stored credentials.
jwt.s1_desc: Four actors in the JWT authentication flow: the Workload (app container in K8s), the K8s API Server (which issues and validates JWTs via its OIDC endpoint), the Secrets Manager JWT Authenticator (validates the token and resolves identity), and the Secrets Vault (stores and returns secret values).
jwt.s2_title: K8s Projects a JWT into the Pod
jwt.s2_desc: When the pod starts, Kubernetes automatically mounts a short-lived ServiceAccount JWT at /var/run/secrets/tokens/jwt. This token is signed by K8s and contains claims about the workload identity: sub (system:serviceaccount:conjur:springboot-app), namespace, serviceaccount name, iss (https://kubernetes.default.svc), and aud (conjur). No manual credential management.
jwt.s3_title: Workload POSTs JWT to Secrets Manager
jwt.s3_desc: The application reads the JWT from its projected volume and sends it to the authn-jwt endpoint: POST /api/authn-jwt/eks-latam/conjur/authenticate. The body contains only the URL-encoded JWT. No password, no API key — the workload's Kubernetes identity is the credential.
jwt.s4_title: Secrets Manager Fetches JWKS from K8s
jwt.s4_desc: Secrets Manager reads the jwks-uri policy variable (pointing to the K8s OIDC discovery URL). It fetches the JSON Web Key Set — the RSA public keys that K8s uses to sign ServiceAccount JWTs. If the signing key changes (K8s key rotation), Secrets Manager automatically fetches the new key when it encounters a token signed with it.
jwt.s5_desc: Using the fetched JWKS, Secrets Manager cryptographically verifies the JWT signature — confirming it was genuinely signed by this K8s cluster. It validates: exp (not expired), iss (matches configured issuer), aud (matches configured audience). A forged token, an expired token, or a token from a different cluster all fail here.
jwt.s6_desc: Secrets Manager extracts the claim specified in token-app-property (e.g. sub). It locates the matching host in policy (host/conjur/authn-jwt/eks-latam/springboot-app) and verifies ALL host annotations against the JWT claims: kubernetes/namespace-name: conjur ✓, kubernetes/service-account-name: springboot-app ✓. Every annotation is a mandatory constraint.
jwt.s7_desc: Authentication succeeds. Secrets Manager issues a short-lived API token — valid for approximately 8 minutes. This token proves the workload's authenticated identity to the Secrets Vault. The original K8s JWT is never stored or forwarded.
jwt.s8_desc: The workload uses the API token to fetch secrets: GET /api/secrets/conjur/variable/... with Authorization: Token token="<api-token>". Secrets Manager enforces policy entitlements. If authorized, the secret value is returned. No credential ever touches source code, a container image, or a CI variable.
jwt.key1_desc: The workload authenticates using its Kubernetes identity — a JWT projected by K8s itself. No API keys, no passwords, no certificates stored in the cluster. If the pod is compromised, there are no credentials to steal.
jwt.key2_desc: The JWT is signed by the Kubernetes API Server's RSA private key. Secrets Manager verifies this signature against the public JWKS before trusting any claims. Identity cannot be forged — only the real K8s cluster can produce a valid signature.
jwt.key3_desc: Every claim-to-identity mapping is declared in Conjur policy YAML and version-controlled. Annotations on host resources define exactly which namespace, ServiceAccount, and pod labels are required. Change the policy to grant or revoke access — no credential rotation needed.

secretshub.title: How CyberArk Secrets Hub Works
secretshub.subtitle: Secrets Hub bridges CyberArk Privilege Cloud and native cloud secret stores — AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, and GCP Secret Manager — so developers keep using native APIs while security teams retain centralized control and automated rotation.
secretshub.s1_desc: Four actors collaborate: Privilege Cloud (PAM) is the source of truth where security teams store, rotate, and audit every secret. Secrets Hub is the SaaS sync engine that reads from PAM and writes to cloud stores. Target Stores (AWS SM, Azure KV, GCP SM) are where developers and workloads consume secrets natively. Workloads read using their existing native SDKs with zero code changes.
secretshub.s2_desc: Security teams store every secret in a PAM Safe inside Privilege Cloud — database passwords, API keys, app tokens, certificates. The Central Policy Manager (CPM) automatically rotates credentials on a schedule defined by password policy. Every rotation is logged, audited, and can be forwarded to SIEM. This is the single source of truth — one place, all policies, full audit trail.
secretshub.s3_title: Establish Trust with Each Target Store
secretshub.s3_desc: An administrator creates a trust relationship between Secrets Hub and each cloud secret store, with no static credentials stored anywhere. For AWS Secrets Manager: an IAM Role is created via a CloudFormation template — Secrets Hub assumes this role to write secrets. For Azure Key Vault: an App Registration with Key Vault Secrets Officer role. For GCP Secret Manager: a Service Account with secretmanager.secretVersionManager permission.
secretshub.s4_title: Grant Secrets Hub Access to PAM Safes
secretshub.s4_desc: The administrator grants the Secrets Hub service user membership to the specific PAM Safes whose secrets should be synced. Secrets Hub only ever reads from PAM — it never writes back. Safe membership follows least-privilege: only the safes explicitly configured for sync are accessible. The Secrets Hub service account gets 'List accounts' and 'Retrieve accounts' permissions, nothing more.
secretshub.s5_desc: A Sync Policy is the rule that links a PAM Safe (source) to one or more target cloud secret stores. You define: which Safe to sync (the filter type is PAM_SAFE), which target stores to push to, and which properties to include (password only, or all secret properties). One policy can fan out to multiple targets simultaneously. Policies are active immediately and can be managed via UI, API, or Terraform provider. Secrets synced to a target store are tagged 'Sourced by CyberArk'.
secretshub.s6_title: Initial Sync — PAM Secrets Land in Cloud Stores
secretshub.s6_desc: Secrets Hub reads the secrets from the configured PAM Safe and writes them to every target store defined in the sync policy. Each synced secret appears in AWS SM, Azure KV, and GCP SM tagged with 'Sourced by CyberArk'. From this moment, developers and workloads can read secrets from their native store using their existing AWS SDK, Azure SDK, or GCP client library calls — nothing changes in application code.
secretshub.s7_title: CPM Rotates → Secrets Hub Re-Syncs Automatically
secretshub.s7_desc: When CPM rotates a credential in PAM (scheduled or on-demand), Secrets Hub detects the change at the next sync cycle. It reads the new secret value from PAM and pushes the updated version to all target stores. AWS SM, Azure KV, and GCP SM all receive the fresh credential. Workloads that read from native stores on next access automatically get the rotated value — no restart, no re-deploy, no notification needed.
secretshub.s8_title: Developers Read From Native Stores — Zero Changes
secretshub.s8_desc: Developers and workloads use native cloud store APIs exactly as they did before. A Lambda function calling secretsmanager.get_secret_value(), a K8s pod reading from Azure Key Vault via CSI driver, a Cloud Run service using the GCP Secret Manager API — all work identically. No CyberArk SDK, no agent, no proxy. Security teams get centralized management and rotation; developers get native experience. Both teams win.
secretshub.key1_desc: Developers keep using native AWS SM, Azure KV, or GCP SM APIs — the same SDKs, the same code, the same IAM patterns. Secrets Hub is invisible to them. Adoption requires no application changes, no new dependencies, no retraining.
secretshub.key2_desc: All secrets originate in and are rotated by CyberArk Privilege Cloud. CPM enforces rotation schedules, password complexity, and reconciliation. Every access and rotation is logged. One audit trail covers secrets consumed across all three cloud providers simultaneously.
secretshub.key3_desc: A single sync policy can push one PAM Safe's secrets to AWS SM, Azure KV, and GCP SM simultaneously. When CPM rotates the credential, all three cloud stores are updated in the same sync cycle. No per-cloud custom scripts, no drift, no stale secrets in any environment.
secretshub.arch1_desc: CyberArk Privilege Cloud (PAM) or PAM Self-Hosted. Secrets live in Safes. CPM handles rotation. Secrets Hub reads with least-privilege membership.
secretshub.arch2_desc: SaaS component of the CyberArk Identity Security Platform. Runs the scan-diff-push-verify sync loop. No secrets stored in Secrets Hub itself.
secretshub.arch3_desc: AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager, or HashiCorp Vault. Trust is established via cloud-native IAM (no stored credentials). Synced secrets are tagged 'Sourced by CyberArk'.
secretshub.arch4_desc: Policies link a PAM Safe (filter: PAM_SAFE) to one or more targets. Configurable via UI, REST API, or Terraform provider. Supports multi-region and cross-account AWS deployments.

jenkins.title: How Jenkins Fetches Secrets From CyberArk
jenkins.subtitle: The Conjur Credentials Plugin lets Jenkins pipelines retrieve secrets from Secrets Manager at runtime using JWT authentication — no API keys stored in Jenkins, no credentials in Jenkinsfiles, no secrets in source code.
jenkins.s1_desc: Five components work together: the Jenkins Server running CI/CD pipelines with the Conjur Credentials Plugin installed, the JWKS Endpoint exposed by Jenkins itself for JWT key verification, Secrets Manager with its JWT Authenticator webservice (authn-jwt/jenkins), and the Secrets Vault storing the actual secret values. No hardcoded credentials exist anywhere in this flow — not in Jenkinsfile, not in Jenkins config, not in environment.
jenkins.s2_desc: Two admins configure the integration once. The Jenkins admin installs the Conjur Credentials Plugin (v3.x, requires Jenkins 2.455+), then in Manage Jenkins → System sets the Conjur URL, account name, and JWT Auth Webservice ID (authn-jwt/jenkins). The Secrets Manager admin creates the JWT authenticator policy with variables: jwks-uri (pointing to Jenkins' JWKS endpoint), token-app-property (e.g. sub), issuer (Jenkins URL), audience (cyberark-conjur), then defines pipeline host identities with annotations (jenkins_pronoun: Pipeline, jenkins_full_name: Project1/pipeline) and grants them access to specific secret variables via !permit blocks.
jenkins.s3_desc: A developer pushes code to the repository or manually triggers a Jenkins build. The Jenkinsfile is committed to source control. It declares credentials via withCredentials([conjurSecretCredential(credentialsId: 'DB_PWD', variable: 'DB_PASSWORD')]) — only the Jenkins Credential ID appears in code. The actual secret path in Secrets Manager, the Conjur URL, and the secret value are all absent from the Jenkinsfile. Security teams own the policy; developers own the pipeline.
jenkins.s4_title: withCredentials Activates the Plugin — JWT Generated
jenkins.s4_desc: When pipeline execution reaches the withCredentials block, the Conjur Credentials Plugin activates. It generates a short-lived JWT (valid only 2 minutes) signed by Jenkins' own RSA private key. The JWT contains: iss (Jenkins URL), aud (cyberark-conjur), sub (the pipeline's jenkins_full_name, e.g. Project1/pipeline), and exp (now + 120 seconds). This JWT is the pipeline's identity credential — there is no API key to store, rotate, or leak. The identity is structural: the job path in Jenkins IS the credential.
jenkins.s5_desc: The plugin POSTs the JWT to Secrets Manager's authn-jwt/jenkins endpoint. Secrets Manager reads its jwks-uri policy variable and fetches the JSON Web Key Set from Jenkins' JWKS endpoint (https://jenkins/jwtauth/conjur-jwk-set). Using the returned RSA public key it cryptographically verifies the JWT signature — confirming the token was genuinely issued by this Jenkins instance. Standard claims are then validated: exp (not expired), iss (matches configured issuer), aud (matches cyberark-conjur).
jenkins.s6_title: Identity Resolved — Policy Check — Vault Access
jenkins.s6_desc: Secrets Manager extracts the sub claim (Project1/pipeline) and maps it via token-app-property to a host in policy. It verifies all host annotations match JWT claims: jenkins_full_name: Project1/pipeline ✓, jenkins_pronoun: Pipeline ✓. Then it checks the !permit block: does this host have read/execute privilege on the requested variable paths? If all pass, a short-lived Conjur API token is issued and the secrets are fetched from the Vault. Each variable access is logged for audit.
jenkins.s7_title: Secrets Returned and Injected as Environment Variables
jenkins.s7_desc: Secrets Manager returns the secret values to the plugin over HTTPS. The plugin injects each value into the build environment under the variable name specified in the Jenkinsfile (DB_PASSWORD, API_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN, etc.). Values exist only in memory for the duration of the withCredentials block scope. They are never written to disk, never appear in build artifacts, and are never stored in Jenkins' credential store in plaintext.
jenkins.s8_title: Pipeline Executes — All Secrets Auto-Masked in Logs
jenkins.s8_desc: The pipeline steps inside withCredentials execute with real secret values available as environment variables. Shell commands, deployment scripts, and test suites use $DB_PASSWORD, $API_KEY normally. Jenkins automatically intercepts all log output and replaces every occurrence of a secret value with ***. Even if a developer accidentally echoes a variable, the log shows ***. When the withCredentials block exits, all values are cleared from memory. The build succeeds with no trace of any credential in logs, artifacts, or source code.
jenkins.key1_desc: No API keys, passwords, or secret paths appear in Jenkinsfile or Jenkins configuration. Pipeline code references only Jenkins Credential IDs (like 'DB_PWD'). Security teams control the Conjur policy and secret paths independently of developers — no coordination needed to rotate.
jenkins.key2_desc: Each build authenticates using a 2-minute JWT signed by Jenkins itself. No stored API key, no long-lived credential, nothing to rotate for the Jenkins identity. If a token is intercepted it expires before it can be reused. The job path in Jenkins (Project1/pipeline) is the identity.
jenkins.key3_desc: Jenkins automatically masks all secret values in build logs. The withCredentials block is the scope boundary — secrets are available only within it and cleared on exit. Three credential tiers (Global, Folder, Job) allow shared secrets across teams with inheritance control via jenkins_pronoun annotations.
jenkins.arch1_desc: Installed via Jenkins Plugin Manager. Bridges Jenkins Credential Store with Secrets Manager. Supports JWT (keyless, recommended) and API key auth. Works with Declarative and Scripted Pipelines, and Freestyle projects.
jenkins.arch2_desc: Jenkins acts as its own OIDC token issuer. Each build generates a scoped JWT signed by Jenkins' private key. Secrets Manager verifies the signature against the public JWKS endpoint. No secret is stored in Jenkins to enable auth.
jenkins.arch3_desc: Three tiers: Global (visible to all jobs), Folder (shared within a folder, inherited by subfolders), Job/Pipeline (job-specific). Each tier maps to a Conjur host with annotation jenkins_pronoun: Global | Folder | Pipeline.
jenkins.arch4_desc: Conjur policy !permit blocks define exactly which pipeline host identities can read which secret variables. Changing access (grant or revoke) is a policy update — no secret rotation, no credential redistribution, immediate effect.

policy.title: How Conjur Policy is Structured
policy.subtitle: Conjur policy is YAML loaded into a tree of branches. Each !policy statement creates a namespace, resources inside get fully-qualified IDs, and roles + permits drive every authorization decision. This animation walks through the structure one concept at a time.
policy.s1_title: Policy as a Tree of Branches
policy.s1_desc: Conjur policy is plain YAML — a list of statements. When loaded, those statements build a tree of branches rooted at a single implicit container: the account itself. Every secret, every identity and every authorization rule lives somewhere on this tree, addressable by its path. The animation will progressively introduce each piece: the root, branches created by !policy, the resources inside them, and how !permit and !grant tie identities to secrets.
policy.s2_desc: A !policy statement at the top of the file creates a top-level branch under the root. It has three keys: id (its name, e.g. prod), owner (a role that controls the branch — usually a group like prod-admins), and body (the list of statements that live inside it). The owner has full authority over everything declared inside the branch and is the only role that can replace or delete its contents.
policy.s3_desc: Branches nest arbitrarily. A !policy declared inside another !policy's body creates a sub-branch. Here, app1 is a sub-branch of prod. Nesting is how teams partition ownership: prod is owned by prod-admins, while app1 can declare its own owner so that the app1 team manages its own resources without touching the rest of prod.
policy.s4_desc: Every resource declared inside a branch is namespaced by it. The !variable db-password declared inside prod/app1 is referenced everywhere as prod/app1/db-password. Two branches can declare the same local name without colliding — they live in different namespaces. The fully-qualified ID is what tools, APIs and audit logs always use.
policy.s5_desc: Roles are anything that can act. !host is a machine identity (a workload, pipeline, server), !user is a person, !group bundles roles together for delegation, and !layer groups hosts. Roles, like resources, get fully-qualified IDs. The !group admins declared inside prod/app1 is prod/app1/admins — that exact identifier is what !permit and !grant refer to.
policy.s6_desc: !permit attaches privileges to a (role, resource) pair. Here, the admins group is permitted read and execute on db-password, meaning anyone in that group can fetch its value. The privilege set is fixed per resource type: variables support read, execute, update; policies support read, create, update, delete. !permit is additive — multiple permits on the same resource simply union the privileges.
policy.s7_desc: !grant adds a member role to a parent role. Granting app1-host membership in admins means app1-host inherits every privilege admins ever had — including the read/execute on db-password from the previous step. This is how indirection works: hosts and users are added to groups; groups are what permits target. To revoke access, change the grant — never touch the permit. Groups can grant into groups; layers can grant into layers; the hierarchy can be as deep as you need.
policy.s8_desc: Putting it all together: branches partition the namespace and ownership, resources live inside branches with fully-qualified IDs, roles identify who can act, !permit names what they can do on each resource, and !grant binds identities into the role hierarchy. Loading this YAML produces exactly the tree on the right, addressable by paths like prod/app1/db-password. Editing the YAML and re-loading is the only way state ever changes — policy is the single source of truth.
policy.key1_desc: Every !policy creates a branch. Resources inside it are addressed as branch/sub-branch/resource. Branches partition ownership: one team can manage prod/app1 without touching prod/app2. Nesting is unbounded — design the tree to match your org.
policy.key2_desc: Roles (!host, !user, !group, !layer) are anything that acts. Resources (!variable, !webservice, !policy) are anything acted upon. !permit connects them with a privilege set, !grant inserts roles into the role hierarchy. Every authorization is computed from this graph.
policy.key3_desc: Policy is plain YAML committed to git. Loading is the only mutation: replace mode overwrites a branch, patch mode adds and removes specific items, update mode adds without deleting. Pull requests review every change to identity and access — auditable, reversible, version-controlled.

footer.desc: A live Kubernetes demo showing four Palo Alto Networks | IDIRA Secrets Manager integration patterns: direct JWT (Spring Boot), Secrets Provider sidecar (.NET), GitHub OIDC, and External Secrets Operator (ESO Shop).
